chunk_summary: This section explores insulin resistance biology in children, emphasizing the adipose-muscle-liver axis, screening pitfalls, and clinical cues for early detection and management during puberty.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of the adipose-muscle-liver axis in pediatric insulin resistance.
Identify clinical cues for insulin resistance, including central adiposity and acanthosis nigricans.
Evaluate the limitations of fasting insulin and HOMA-IR as screening tools.
Apply clinical guidelines for dysglycemia, dyslipidemia, and liver risk assessment in children.
key_facts: Visceral and ectopic fat drive hepatic de novo lipogenesis and impair insulin signaling.
Puberty introduces physiologic insulin resistance, requiring earlier follow-up for borderline abnormalities.
High-yield clinical cues include central adiposity, acanthosis nigricans, elevated BP, and dyslipidemia (high triglycerides, low HDL-C).
Fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are unreliable for diagnosing prediabetes or T2DM due to variability.
Case vignette: A 12-year-old with central adiposity and acanthosis nigricans should undergo standardized anthropometry, BP measurement, and bundled screening for dysglycemia and liver risk.
reference_quote: Major guidance emphasizes that fasting insulin is highly variable and does not reliably diagnose prediabetes or T2DM, and its use can lead to false labeling and unnecessary anxiety. (Source: NASPGHAN Clinical Practice Guideline)
document_key_topics: Screening pitfalls for pediatric metabolic risk
Clinical cues for early detection of dysglycemia
Impact of puberty on insulin resistance
